narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is a bit above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 43, well above the usual 36.70. You're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 77.4% probability that you would fail it. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed on the bar and graduate strong. The model's decision hinged mostly on your academic deciles from earlier years: your Year 3 school decile of 2 was the biggest factor pushing toward a failure prediction, as it signals performance in the lower end compared to the average decile of about 5.5. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about consistency in your grades relative to peers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success and showing your potential in a key area where you exceed most applicants. Compared to typical students, your early deciles are notably lower than averages around 5-6, which overshadowed your strengths and tipped the scales. This doesn't reflect your full story or determination, especially given challenges many Black students like yourself face in competitive academic tracks. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as those proved most influential here. You might also seek targeted LSAT or bar prep resources to build on your already strong score. Reapplying after addressing these academic gaps could shift the prediction in your favor—many students improve and succeed. Keep pushing; your background shows resilience that numbers can't fully capture.
